summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

A coin is flipped and a die is rolled. What is P(heads AND 4)?

Independent events: P = 1/2 × 1/6 = 1/12.

A bag has 5 red and 3 blue marbles. You draw one, do NOT replace it, then draw another. What is P(red then blue)?

Without replacement (dependent): P = 5/8 × 3/7 = 15/56.

A spinner has 4 equal sections. You spin twice. What is P(red both times)?

Independent: P = 1/4 × 1/4 = 1/16.

From a deck of 52 cards, you draw 2 cards without replacement. What is P(ace then ace)?

Dependent: P = 4/52 × 3/51 = 12/2652 = 1/221.

Flipping a coin 3 times: what is P(all heads)?

Independent: P = 1/2 × 1/2 × 1/2 = 1/8.

What is the 'multiplication rule' for probability?

The multiplication rule finds P(A and B). For independent events: multiply directly. For dependent events: use conditional probability P(B|A).

What is the 'addition rule' for mutually exclusive events?

For mutually exclusive events: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). Since they can't overlap, there's no double-counting.

What is a 'tree diagram' used for in probability?

A tree diagram branches out at each stage of an experiment, showing all possible paths and helping organize compound probability calculations.

A box contains 6 red and 4 blue socks. You pick two socks without looking and without replacement. What is P(both red)?

Dependent: P = 6/10 × 5/9 = 30/90 = 1/3.

A student guesses on two independent multiple-choice questions, each with 4 choices. What is P(both correct)?

Independent: P = 1/4 × 1/4 = 1/16.

A candy dish has 10 chocolates and 5 mints. You take one, eat it, then take another. What is P(chocolate then mint)?

Without replacement: P = 10/15 × 5/14 = 50/210 = 5/21.

A traffic light has a 60% chance of being green. You encounter two independent traffic lights. What is P(both green)?

Independent: P = 0.60 × 0.60 = 0.36 = 36%.

A basketball player makes 80% of free throws. What is the probability of making 3 in a row (independent shots)?

Independent: P = 0.8 × 0.8 × 0.8 = 0.512 = 51.2%.

A shelf has 8 fiction and 4 non-fiction books. You randomly select 2 without replacement. What is P(both fiction)?

Dependent: P = 8/12 × 7/11 = 56/132 = 14/33.

It rains 40% of days in April. Assuming independence, what is the probability of rain on both April 1 and April 2?

Independent: P = 0.40 × 0.40 = 0.16 = 16%.

A class has 15 boys and 10 girls. Two students are randomly chosen without replacement to lead a project. What is P(boy then girl)?

Dependent: P = 15/25 × 10/24 = 150/600 = 1/4.

A medical test has a 95% accuracy rate. For two independent patients, what is P(both tests are accurate)?

Independent: P = 0.95 × 0.95 = 0.9025 = 90.25%.

A jar has 7 green and 3 yellow marbles. You draw 3 marbles without replacement. What is P(all green)?

Dependent: P = 7/10 × 6/9 × 5/8 = 210/720 = 7/24.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: Drawing a card, replacing it, then drawing again is an example of:

With replacement, the deck returns to its original state, so the draws are independent.

Example: What is the 'multiplication rule' for probability?

The multiplication rule finds P(A and B). For independent events: multiply directly. For dependent events: use conditional probability P(B|A).

Example: A student guesses on two independent multiple-choice questions, each with 4 choices. What is P(both correct)?

Independent: P = 1/4 × 1/4 = 1/16.
